metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `novelty_drop_threshold` and `novelty_priority_floor` belong in `src/rebar/config.py` (VerifyConfig / stdlib core) or in the `[tool.rebar.llm]` reserved section (read by `rebar.llm.config._read_llm_file_table`) — the established home for LLM-gate calibration parameters — and update the AC and implementation plan accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan adds three new `verify.*` config keys (`novelty_drop_threshold`, `novelty_priority_floor`, `novelty_drop_active`) to the CORE stdlib `VerifyConfig` dataclass in `src/rebar/config.py`, which already governs non-LLM gate operations. Two of the three (`novelty_drop_threshold` and `novelty_priority_floor`) are LLM-tier calibration scalars specific to one experimental sub-path of one gate. The codebase already has a distinct resolved section for LLM-gate-specific parameters: the `[tool.rebar.llm]` reserved section read by `rebar.llm.config` (via `_read_llm_file_table`). Placing LLM-calibration floats in the stdlib core (which is 'dependency-free' and 'never depends on the agents extra' per `config.py` line 124) couples a still-inert experimental LLM feature into the core config surface unnecessarily. The prior precedent `verify.verify_window_headroom` has direct production call sites in `verify.py` and `workflow_ops.py`; the three novelty keys will be inert by default.
findings.location: AC line 2 ('`verify.novelty_drop_threshold` and `verify.novelty_priority_floor` … committed config-overridable defaults in `src/rebar/config.py`'); `src/rebar/config.py` VerifyConfig dataclass

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS`, `decide.novelty`, and `verify.score_novelty` are actually present in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py` and `verify.py` on the work branch before implementing the drop predicate; if absent, explicitly scope their creation into this task or block on 150b deliverables.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS`, `decide.novelty`, and `verify.score_novelty` are '150b deliverables already merged on this branch', but none of these symbols exist in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py` or `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` (or anywhere under `src/rebar/llm`). Additionally, `plan-review-novelty.eval.yaml` is absent from `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/` and `NOVELTY_EXPECTS` is absent from `eval_scorers.py`. The plan's activation guard and AC#1 drop predicate both depend on consuming `decide.novelty` / `verify.score_novelty` as pre-existing entry points.
findings.location: Plan preamble: 'the task-150b novelty score (`decide.novelty` / `verify.score_novelty`, both landed by 150b)'; AC #1, AC #3, AC #4, AC #8
findings.suggested_fix: Add a branch-state check step: run `grep -n 'NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS\|def novelty' src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py` and `grep -n 'score_novelty' src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` on the work branch. If absent, either (a) list them as explicit sub-deliverables of this task with their own ACs, or (b) add a hard dependency on 150b being merged first.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that ec89's `remediation_mode` config key is present in `src/rebar/config.py`'s `VerifyConfig` and that `coverage['remediation']` is populated by ec89's `_run_plan_review` wiring before implementing the triple-gate guard in this task.
findings.finding: The plan asserts it 'consumes task ec89's remediation-mode eligibility decision (`coverage["remediation"]`); does NOT re-implement the freshness-window/precondition check' — but ec89's primary deliverable (`remediation_mode` config key in `src/rebar/config.py` and `remediation_mode_candidate` in `attest.py`) is absent from the codebase. `VerifyConfig` in `config.py` has no `remediation_mode` field, and `attest.py` has no `remediation_mode_candidate` function.
findings.location: AC #7: 'Consumes task ec89's remediation-mode eligibility decision (`coverage["remediation"]`); does NOT re-implement the freshness-window/precondition check'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that 150b's `decide.novelty`, `verify.score_novelty`, `NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS` (in `decide.py`), and the `plan_review_novelty` contract alias (in `passes.py`) are actually present on the working branch before implementing any AC in this task.
findings.finding: The plan states that 150b deliverables (`NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS`, `decide.novelty`, `verify.score_novelty`, and the `plan_review_novelty` contract alias in `passes.py`) 'are already merged on this branch', but none of these symbols exist anywhere in the visible codebase. AC1 requires `rising_floor_drop` to call `decide.novelty`; AC8 requires the wrapper to consume `verify.score_novelty` via an injected novelty map. If 150b has not actually landed on the working branch, every AC in this task that touches novelty scores is unimplementable.
findings.location: Plan preamble ('Note: NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS, decide.novelty, and verify.score_novelty are 150b deliverables already merged on this branch'); AC1, AC8

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing `decide.rising_floor_drop`, verify that `NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS` and `decide.novelty()` (from task 150b) exist in the working branch's `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py`, and that `verify.score_novelty` exists in `verify.py`; if absent, add their creation to this task's scope or block on 150b delivery.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `decide.novelty` and `verify.score_novelty` are 'both landed by 150b' and immediately consumable, but codebase searches return zero hits for `NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS`, `score_novelty`, or any novelty arithmetic in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py` or `verify.py` — the upstream API this entire task's AC1/drop arithmetic depends on is unverified as present in the implementation branch.
findings.location: Plan preamble: 'Consume the task-150b novelty score (`decide.novelty` / `verify.score_novelty`, both landed by 150b)'; AC #1 (`src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] After moving dropped findings in the wrapper, also update `verdict['coverage']['counts']['advisory_surfaced']` (subtract N dropped) and `verdict['coverage']['counts']['dropped']` (add N dropped) so the counts record stays consistent with the actual advisory/dropped lists and the attestation manifest encodes the post-floor advisory count.
findings.finding: The wrapper mutates `verdict['advisory']` and `verdict['dropped']` AFTER `finalize_verdict` has already baked `coverage['counts']['advisory_surfaced']` and `coverage['counts']['dropped']` into the verdict. The attestation manifest (`attest.sign_plan_review`) binds `advisory: {counts.get('advisory_surfaced', 0)}` (attest.py line 173), so the signed manifest will encode the pre-floor advisory count — inconsistent with the actual floor-modified advisory list. Any consumer comparing `len(verdict['advisory'])` to `coverage['counts']['advisory_surfaced']` (e.g. an integrity check or a downstream analytics query) will observe a mismatch.
findings.location: `_run_plan_review` wrapper, post-verdict floor application; `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` lines 428-434; `attest.py` line 173
findings.suggested_fix: After the floor loop, patch the counts: `cov = verdict.setdefault('coverage', {}); counts = cov.setdefault('counts', {}); n = len(floored); counts['advisory_surfaced'] = counts.get('advisory_surfaced', 0) - n; counts['dropped'] = counts.get('dropped', 0) + n`. Then set the narrowed/floored_* keys as planned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the 150b novelty sub-call is per-finding or batched (and if per-finding, cap the droppable-surface size or require batching), and add a per-review cost/latency note to the AC or config doc.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a '150b sub-call' for novelty scoring inside `_run_plan_review` but does not specify whether novelty is scored per-finding (O(N) LLM calls per review) or in a single batched call, nor does it state a per-review latency or cost target for this path.
findings.location: ## Where the floor is applied — 'scores novelty over the surfaced advisory findings … via the 150b sub-call'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-novelty.eval.yaml` (a 150b deliverable) exists on the working branch, or explicitly note that this task must create it if 150b's file_impact placement was wrong.
findings.finding: The plan's activation guard references `rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty` as the operator-facing check that the 150b `discriminates_novelty` eval has cleared its bar, but the eval spec file `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-novelty.eval.yaml` does not exist in the repo. Without this spec the command fails, making the documented activation procedure non-functional.
findings.location: Activation guard section ('rebar prompt eval plan-review-novelty → the labeled pairs discriminate at gate: at_least(2)/epochs:3 + coverage_threshold:0.8'); AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify WHERE the ec89 `coverage["remediation"]` eligibility key is produced: name the workflow step, workflow_op, or config check that populates it, so the wrapper can reliably read `coverage['remediation']['eligible']`.
findings.finding: The plan references `coverage["remediation"]` as the ec89 eligibility key — 'Consumes task ec89's remediation-mode eligibility decision (`coverage["remediation"]`)' — but this key does not exist anywhere in the codebase. Neither `remediation_mode`, `coverage["remediation"]`, nor any ec89 eligibility field is present in any coverage-assembly, `finalize_verdict`, or `partition_findings` path.
findings.location: AC line 7: 'Consumes task ec89's remediation-mode eligibility decision (`coverage["remediation"]`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact `coverage` key name ec89 emits for its eligibility decision (either by reading the landed ec89 code on the working branch or coordinating with ec89's author), and document it as a named constant shared between ec89 and this task.
findings.finding: The plan consumes ec89's remediation-mode eligibility decision via `coverage['remediation']`, but ec89's code deliverables (`remediation_mode` config key in `config.py`, `remediation_mode_candidate` in `attest.py`, and the wiring in `_run_plan_review`) are absent from the visible codebase. The exact key name `coverage['remediation']` is an assumption — ec89's AC text says 'record decision on coverage' without naming the key. If ec89's key name differs (e.g., `coverage['remediation_eligible']`), this task silently falls back to always-full-review.
findings.location: AC7 ('Consumes task ec89's remediation-mode eligibility decision (`coverage["remediation"]`)'); plan section 'Activation guard'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output schema of the `verify.score_novelty` / `decide.novelty` sub-call: what key(s) hold the per-finding novelty float(s), what the keying convention is (by `id`, `index`, or `norm_id`), and what value is substituted for a finding with no score.
findings.finding: The plan references `decide.novelty` and `verify.score_novelty` (task-150b deliverables) as the novelty-scoring sub-call, but never specifies the OUTPUT SCHEMA of that sub-call: what key holds the float novelty score, what the numeric range/contract is, and how a missing or partial result (e.g., only some advisory findings scored) is handled. The wrapper must key into the returned structure to extract per-finding novelty floats, but the plan leaves that extraction contract implicit.
findings.location: Plan section 'Where the floor is applied' / AC line 1 (`decide.rising_floor_drop`)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure mode for the 150b novelty sub-call inside `_run_plan_review`: define whether a sub-call failure falls back to un-floored behavior (recommended), raises with a clear error, or retries — and ensure unscored findings default to carryover (novelty=0) so the predicate is total.
findings.finding: The novelty sub-call (150b LLM call invoked inside _run_plan_review when the gate is active) has no stated failure mode or graceful-degradation path. If the sub-call times out, errors, or returns a malformed score map, the plan does not specify whether the wrapper (a) propagates the exception and fails the whole review, (b) falls back to un-floored behavior, or (c) surfaces a partial/empty dropped set.
findings.location: 'Where the floor is applied' section + AC line 4 ('floor is applied in _run_plan_review')
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the novelty sub-call in a try/except; on any exception or missing score, log a warning and fall back to un-floored verdict (treat all findings as carryover). Document the fallback in the AC so the test can assert it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior when the novelty-scoring sub-call fails or returns an unusable result: the plan should explicitly state that the wrapper degrades to the un-narrowed (full advisory) verdict and records the failure in coverage, rather than crashing the review.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback for a failed novelty-scoring sub-call. The wrapper calls the 150b sub-call after verdict production and before sidecar emission; if the sub-call raises (LLM unavailable, network error, malformed output), the plan is silent on whether the wrapper (a) aborts and surfaces an error, (b) emits the un-narrowed verdict, or (c) skips the floor silently.
findings.location: Plan section 'Where the floor is applied (Pass-3 stays pure)' / AC line 8 (offline tests)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'If the novelty sub-call raises or returns no usable scores, the wrapper degrades gracefully: the advisory list is left un-narrowed, `narrowed: false` is recorded on coverage, and the failure is logged (matching the sidecar best-effort pattern).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that any downstream CI job or tooling that consumes `plan_review_impact_distribution.py --json` output is not broken by adding percentile keys to the `impact` dict (additive change), and document that the `p40` key name matches what this task will use.
findings.finding: `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` exists but currently outputs only mean, median, min, max, and `pct_critical_ge_0.75` — it does NOT print any percentiles (p40 or otherwise). The plan states the script 'is extended to print the impact PERCENTILES (incl. p40)' to make the floor value reproducible. This is a legitimate in-scope edit target but the plan does not enumerate which other callers or CI consumers of the script's output would be affected by a new output field.
findings.location: AC2 ('scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py prints the impact percentiles the floor is derived from')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `statistics.quantiles(impacts, n=10)[3]` (the p40 = 4th decile) to `impact_stats` and render it in `render_text`; the change is additive to the JSON shape.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` extension (adding p40 percentile output) is explicitly scoped as a deliverable of this task in AC2, and that the AC is worded as a creation ('extend to print percentiles') not a pre-existing capability ('prints percentiles').
findings.finding: AC2 states that `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` 'prints the impact PERCENTILES (incl. p40)' to make the floor derivation reproducible — but the script currently outputs only `n/mean/median/min/max/pct_critical_ge_0.75` with no percentile/quantile/p40 computation, making the reproducibility claim an unverified capability assertion rather than a deliverable already present.
findings.location: Plan section 'Defaults this task owns': 'scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py is extended to print the impact PERCENTILES (incl. p40)'; AC #2
findings.suggested_fix: Add `statistics.quantiles(impacts, n=10)[3]` (p40 = the 4th decile) to `impact_stats` and emit it in `render_text`; update AC2 to read 'extend `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` to compute and print the p40 impact percentile'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` to compute and print impact percentiles (at minimum p25/p40/p50/p75) so the committed `novelty_priority_floor` value is reproducibly derivable from the corpus, not just asserted — the script currently has no quantile computation.
findings.finding: scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py does not compute any impact percentiles (no quantile/p40 logic exists), yet AC2 and the 'Defaults this task owns' section assert it 'prints the impact PERCENTILES (incl. p40)' as the reproducible, verifiable basis for the novelty_priority_floor value.
findings.location: AC #2 / 'Defaults this task owns' section / scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `statistics.quantiles(impacts, n=10)` (or manual sorted-index computation for stdlib compat) block to `aggregate()` and surface p10/p25/p40/p50/p75/p90 in both the JSON output and the `render_text` report.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` to compute and print impact percentiles (at minimum p25, p40, p50, p75) so the `verify.novelty_priority_floor` default is reproducible from the corpus, not asserted.
findings.finding: `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` currently reports `mean`, `median`, `min`, `max`, and `pct_critical_ge_0.75` for the impact distribution, but does NOT print percentiles (p10, p25, p40, p75, etc.). The plan says this script 'is extended to print the impact PERCENTILES (incl. p40) so the chosen number is reproducible, not asserted.' This is a future extension described as if already present, but the AC requires it to be committed. An executor reading the script as-is cannot reproduce the p40 floor value.
findings.location: AC line 2: '`scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` prints the impact percentiles the floor is derived from'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `statistics.quantiles(impacts, n=100)` (or equivalent) to `aggregate()` and render p25/p40/p50/p75 in both the text and JSON outputs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `verify.novelty_priority_floor` is derived from the **priority** distribution (validity×impact) or the **impact** distribution, and update either the Defaults section derivation method or the Drop rule predicate so both sections reference the same scale; update `plan_review_impact_distribution.py` to print percentiles of the operative quantity (priority) if that is the intended comparand.
findings.finding: The floor (`verify.novelty_priority_floor`) is derived from the p40 **impact** percentile (Defaults section) but the drop predicate compares it against **priority = validity × impact** (Drop rule section). These are on different scales: a finding with impact above p40 but low validity could have priority below the floor and be dropped, while the floor calibration gives no signal about that. The plan's own explanation ('the validity factor is already in priority') does not resolve the scale mismatch — it describes why the floor doesn't need to be per-finding, not why an impact-percentile floor is the correct comparand for a validity-weighted product. An implementer reading the two sections independently will derive the floor number correctly (p40 of impact) but apply it to a different distribution (priority), making the floor's intended operating point unverifiable.
findings.location: Defaults section ('verify.novelty_priority_floor = a scalar constant … set to the p40 impact percentile') vs. Drop rule section ('priority = validity × impact … the drop test is priority < floor')
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) derive the floor from the **priority** distribution (extend the script to print priority = validity × impact percentiles, and set the floor to p40 of priority) so the comparand and the floor are on the same scale; or (b) change the predicate to compare `impact < floor` (raw impact, not validity-weighted), which is consistent with an impact-percentile floor but requires rewriting the AC and drop rule to use `impact` directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal empirical probe — even a one-shot histogram of `decide.novelty` scores over the 245-event corpus advisory findings — to ground T_novel=0.7 before first operator enablement, or document the measurement that would let an operator choose their own value at activation time.
findings.finding: T_novel=0.7 is a stated policy default with no measurement or spike planned to validate it produces the intended precision tradeoff; per-corpus calibration is explicitly deferred to a future epic with no interim probe.
findings.location: ## Defaults this task owns — `verify.novelty_drop_threshold` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` (already being modified) to also print the distribution of novelty scores across the corpus's advisory findings; annotate what fraction of findings fall above 0.7 so the operator has a concrete data point when they flip the gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) requiring a CLAUDE.md / ADR update that documents the rising-floor drop pattern, the triple-gate activation contract, and the conditional verdict shape (`dropped`, `narrowed`) for future maintainers.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline stage (the rising-floor drop wrapper), a new verdict shape (`dropped` bucket, `narrowed` key on `coverage`), and new config keys — a novel architectural decision — but no AC or plan step calls for updating CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or a design doc to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria (all ACs) / ## Where the floor is applied

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an offline test covering a partial/missing novelty map (some finding IDs absent from the injected map) and the zero/None novelty edge case, asserting a defined fallback contract (e.g., absent novelty → treat as carryover / preserve, never raise KeyError) for the `_run_plan_review` wrapper.
findings.finding: The offline test suite covers the four predicate quadrants and the inert-guard path but does not specify any test for the invalid/absent novelty-score case — i.e., when the injected novelty map is missing an entry for one or more findings (the 150b sub-call returns a partial map or errors). Without this, the wrapper could silently drop a finding (treating missing as 0.0 < T_novel, preserving it) or raise a KeyError, and neither outcome is locked down by a test.
findings.location: AC — last bullet: 'Offline tests … the pure drop predicate (all four quadrants) … with an INJECTED novelty map'; 'Drop rule' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the injected-novelty-map test fixture, include a case where one finding's ID is absent from the map and assert the wrapper preserves that finding (treat missing novelty as 0 / below T_novel). Document the fallback in the predicate's docstring so the contract is explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the ordering and atomicity contract for the advisory-to-dropped mutation vs. the sidecar write: either build the new verdict dict (with dropped populated) before mutating advisory (copy-then-swap), or specify that a sidecar write failure restores the original advisory list, so findings are never silently lost.
findings.finding: The in-memory mutation sequence (moving findings from advisory into the dropped bucket) is not specified as atomic with respect to the sidecar write. If the sidecar write fails after the advisory list has been mutated, the caller receives a verdict with findings silently absent from advisory but not durably recorded in the sidecar's dropped bucket — a silent data loss window.
findings.location: 'Narrowed coverage + sidecar (observability)' section + AC line 6
findings.suggested_fix: Construct the narrowed verdict as a new object (copy advisory, populate dropped) without mutating the original; only replace the live verdict reference after the sidecar write succeeds. This makes the operation safe under sidecar failure.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
